one_line_takeaway: MemJack shows that a memory-augmented multi-agent pipeline can turn ordinary, unmodified natural images into transferable jailbreak anchors against VLMs, reaching 71.48% ASR on COCO val2017 against Qwen3-VL-Plus (up to 90% with more rounds) and far outperforming text-only and static multimodal jailbreak baselines, with cross-image memory reuse (removed in ablation: 72%->38% ASR) as the dominant driver of effectiveness.
key_finding: On the full 5,000-image COCO val2017 run against Qwen3-VL-Plus, MemJack achieves 71.48% ASR with a mean of 5.18 rounds to success (68.3% of successes within 6 rounds, 89.1% within 10); extending the budget to R=100 on a 100-image subset raises ASR to 90%. Cross-dataset ASR ranges 62-91% across seven additional benchmarks (e.g., FigStep images 91%, VLBreakBench 73%, SIUO 63.37%). Cross-model ASR (100-image subset) ranges from 35% (Gemini-3-Flash) to 82% (Mistral-Medium-3), with Qwen3-VL-Plus at 72%, Llama-3.2-11B-Vision 68%, GLM-4.6-V-Flash 63%, Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct 53%. Baseline comparison on the 100-image COCO subset: GCG 18% (white-box), AutoDAN-Turbo 30% (white-box), Visual-Adv 17% (white-box), HADES 10%, FigStep 13%, QR-Attack 1% (all black-box) versus MemJack 53% (white-box) / 72% (black-box). Ablation on the same subset: removing Memory drops ASR from 72% to 38% and nearly doubles average rounds (5.38 to 9.11) -- the single largest component; removing Reflection drops ASR to 67% (avg rounds 6.19); removing Dynamic Replanning drops ASR to 66% (avg rounds 6.27). Memory index growth over the full campaign: Visual index reaches 65,973 entries, Strategy index 22,521 (~3:1 ratio); Effect-value of new entries stays stable at 0.30-0.38 (no quality degradation); reuse ratio 6.2x (65,973 entries / 10,599 unique anchor-goal keys); ASR by anchor experience shows an inverted-U, peaking at 14.6% for anchors with 13-20 memory entries and dropping to 3.6% beyond 20+ entries. Attack-angle usage (15,090 rounds): Visual Intuitive Association 28.4%, Practical Knowledge 23.6%, Hypothetical Reasoning 23.2% (jointly >74%); angle-switching fires in 32.0% of successes, anchor replanning in 56.1%, and 27.4% of the 3,574 successful COCO attacks originate from reflection-corrected near-miss prompts. Defense-pattern distribution (19,105 rounds): Direct refusal 41.4% (most common but easiest to bypass), Safe answer 27.5%, Benign reframing 20.8% (rated most resilient to bypass). The linear-separability probe found an 83.8%+-0.5% stratified 5-fold SVM accuracy distinguishing safe/unsafe embeddings on 17,845 JailBreakV pairs, used to justify the INLP filter. MemJack-Bench totals 113,092 trajectories (8,147 Unsafe / 16,570 Controversial / 88,375 Safe); using its largest jailbroken subset (COCO-Jailbreak, N=3,574) to re-probe ten victim models shows static benchmarks (AdvBench-M, SIUO, JailbreakV) yield near-zero ASR on most API models while COCO-Jailbreak differentiates them sharply (e.g., Mistral-Medium-3 49.69%, LLaVA-v1.6-Vicuna-7B 43.73%, Kimi-K2.5 28.96%, Claude-Haiku-4.5 2.69%, GPT-5-Mini 0.25%, Qwen3-VL-Plus itself 61.78% on the dataset it was generated against).
core_contribution: The paper introduces MemJack, a memory-augmented multi-agent jailbreak attack framework against Vision-Language Models (VLMs) that exploits the semantic content of unmodified, benign natural images (rather than pixel perturbations, typography, or overtly harmful imagery) as reusable 'attack anchors.' MemJack runs a three-agent closed loop per image: (1) a Vulnerability Planning/Strategic Planning Agent maps visual anchors in the image to malicious intents and safety-policy categories; (2) an Iterative Attack Agent crafts adversarial prompts through six complementary 'attack angles' (Visual Intuitive Association, Scenario Story Extension, First-Person Role Perspective, Hypothetical Reasoning, Practical Knowledge Exploration, Contextual Dialogue), optionally refined via evolutionary search or MCTS, and pre-screens candidates with an Iterative Nullspace Projection (INLP)-based null-space filter that estimates a 'refusal residue' in a shared multimodal embedding space to reduce premature refusals; (3) an Evaluation & Feedback Agent scores the victim's response with a Safety Guard (Qwen3Guard-Gen) and, on failure, a Reflection module classifies the defense pattern and either repairs the prompt (near-miss exploitation) or triggers replanning with a new anchor. Across images, an Experience-Driven Memory Module (FAISS-indexed Multimodal Experience Memory plus a Multimodal Jailbreak Knowledge Graph) persists successful (anchor, goal, strategy) tuples and causal anchor-strategy-defense relationships, letting later attacks reuse strategies learned on earlier images. The paper also releases MemJack-Bench, a dataset of >113,000 (image, prompt, response, safety-label) attack trajectories collected during the experiments.
threat_model: Attacker interacts with a target/victim VLM (black-box API access for commercial models such as Qwen3-VL-Plus, Gemini-3-Flash, GPT-5-Mini, Claude-Haiku-4.5, DeepSeek-V3.2, Mistral-Medium-3, Kimi-K2.5; white-box/local access for open-source models such as Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, Llama-3.2-11B-Vision-Instruct, LLaVA-v1.6-Vicuna-7B, GLM-4.6-V-Flash) using only unmodified, original photographs plus natural-language text prompts -- no pixel perturbation, no adversarial image construction, no typographic embedding. The attacker has an automated pipeline (its own VLM agents, an embedding model for the INLP filter, and a safety-judge model) and a bounded query budget (default R=20 rounds per image, extended to R=100 in one experiment). Goal: elicit at least one response the safety judge labels 'Unsafe' within the round budget, across eight harmful categories (e.g., Non-violent/Violent Illegal Acts, Unethical Acts, Politically Sensitive Topics, PII, Suicide & Self-Harm, Sexual Content, Copyright Violation). This is a red-teaming/attack paper studying vulnerability of deployed VLM safety alignment to semantically-camouflaged multi-turn attacks grounded in benign imagery.
limitations: The paper's own framing rather than an independent critique: (1) the strongest generalization numbers (COCO-Jailbreak transferability, Table 6) reuse prompts/trajectories originally optimized against Qwen3-VL-Plus and replay them statically against other models, which is a different (weaker, non-adaptive) evaluation than MemJack's normal adaptive closed loop against those models -- the paper does not flag this as a limitation but it is a methodological asymmetry evident from the setup description. (2) Attack success and the entire pipeline depend on an automated LLM-based judge (Qwen3Guard-Gen) rather than human evaluation of the harm/quality of elicited content; no human-annotation validation of the judge's labels is reported. (3) The INLP null-space filter and linear-separability claim are validated only via 5-fold SVM accuracy (83.8%) on JailBreakV pairs, not via ablation showing the filter's marginal contribution to final ASR (this ablation is not in Table 5, which only covers Memory/Reflection/Replanning). (4) The paper explicitly notes an inverted-U in anchor-experience-vs-ASR (peaking at 13-20 entries, declining beyond 20+) suggesting diminishing/negative returns from overly generic memory entries, which it does not fully resolve. (5) No defensive countermeasure or mitigation is proposed or evaluated against MemJack itself; the paper's 'implications' (Section 5.3.2) only speculate that VLM defenses should prioritize benign reframing over direct refusal.

datasets_used: COCO val2017 (5,000 images, 15 scene categories) - primary large-scale evaluation
COCO train2017 150-image stratified subset (10 images x 15 categories)
COCO val2017 100-image stratified subset (sampled proportional to ~71% ASR: 71 success + 29 failure) - used for cross-model, ablation, baseline comparisons
JailbreakV-RedTeam2K (N=2,000 full; N=100 subset used in some tables)
JailBreakV pairs (N=17,845 image-prompt embeddings) - used for the linear-separability/INLP probe
MemJack-Bench (released dataset, N=113,092 trajectories: 8,147 Unsafe / 16,570 Controversial / 88,375 Safe)
COCO-Jailbreak (N=3,574) - jailbroken subset of MemJack-Bench used for cross-model transferability test
quantitative_results: Overall ASR on COCO val2017 (N=5000, R=20) against Qwen3-VL-Plus: 71.48%, mean 5.18 rounds to success
ASR extended to R=100 on 100-image COCO subset: 90%, avg rounds 9.72
ASR on 100-image COCO val2017 subset, R=20: 72%, avg rounds 5.38
Cross-dataset ASR range: 62-91% (COCO train2017 subset 65.33%, MMBench 66.00%, JailbreakV-RedTeam2K 66.00%, SIUO 63.37%, MM-SafetyBench 62.00%, VLBreakBench 73.00%, FigStep 91.00%)
Cross-model ASR (100-image subset): Qwen3-VL-Plus 72%, Gemini-3-Flash 35%, Mistral-Medium-3 82%, Llama-3.2-11B-Vision 68%, Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct 53%, GLM-4.6-V-Flash 63%

Baseline comparison (100-image COCO subset): GCG 18% (white-box, text-only), AutoDAN-Turbo 30% (white-box, text-only), Visual-Adv 17% (white-box), HADES 10% (black-box), FigStep 13% (black-box), QR-Attack 1% (black-box), MemJack 53% (white-box) / 72% (black-box)
Ablation on 100-image COCO subset: full MemJack 72% ASR / 5.38 avg rounds; w/o Memory 38% / 9.11; w/o Reflection 67% / 6.19; w/o Replanning 66% / 6.27
Linear SVM on top-50 PCA of 17,845 JailBreakV image-prompt embeddings: 83.8% +/- 0.5% stratified 5-fold accuracy (9,756 safe / 8,089 unsafe)

Memory index growth over full campaign: Visual index 65,973 entries, Strategy index 22,521 entries (~3:1 ratio); reuse ratio 6.2x (65,973/10,599 unique anchor-goal keys); Effect-value of new entries stable at 0.30-0.38
ASR by anchor memory-experience level: peaks at 14.6% for 13-20 entries, drops to 3.6% for 20+ entries (inverted-U)

Attack angle usage (15,090 rounds): Visual Intuitive Association 28.4%, Practical Knowledge 23.6%, Hypothetical Reasoning 23.2%, First-Person Role 8.6%, Scenario Story Extension 4.9%, Contextual Dialogue 2.8%
Defense pattern distribution (19,105 rounds): Direct refusal 41.4%, Safe answer 27.5%, Benign reframing 20.8%, Uncategorized 9.8%, Topic shift 0.3%, Preaching 0.1%
Angle-switching fires in 32.0% of successful attacks; anchor replanning in 56.1%; 27.4% of the 3,574 COCO successes attributed to reflection-corrected prompts
Scene-category heatmap: Office workspace scenes concentrate Non-violent Illegal Acts at 81.2% ASR contribution; Street/traffic 61.7%, Transportation 60.3%

MemJack-Bench COCO-Jailbreak (N=3,574) cross-model ASR: Qwen3-VL-Plus 61.78%, Mistral-Medium-3 49.69%, LLaVA-v1.6-Vicuna-7B 43.73%, Kimi-K2.5 28.96%, DeepSeek-V3.2 48.07%, Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct 25.24%, Llama-3.2-11B-Vision 23.81%, Gemini-3-Flash 14.55%, Claude-Haiku-4.5 2.69%, GPT-5-Mini 0.25%; static benchmarks (AdvBench-M, SIUO, JailbreakV) yield near-zero ASR for most API models on the same models
baselines_compared: Visual-Adv / Visual Adversarial Examples (white-box, pixel perturbation)
